title: Born in 1939 to a Professor's Family
summary: Garman Robinson was born in New York City in 1939 to a father who rose from a Pennsylvania German farm to a physics and chemistry professorship at NYU, and a working-class mother of Irish and German descent who was the first in her family to attend college.

title: A Wartime Childhood in Cambridge
summary: During World War II the family moved to Cambridge so her father could work at the MIT radiation lab, and she remembers the air raids and blackout curtains she later connected to her anti-war feeling, before a move to suburban Pleasantville.

title: A Father Tormented by Corporate Life
summary: She recalls her father coming home anguished by the backbiting of corporate science, a memory that fixed her and her brother against corporate America, alongside her mother's deep prejudice against anyone of color.

title: The Managerial Scientist and Class Unease
summary: Garman Robinson describes her father's managerial role atop his scientific knowledge, the cutthroat competition from non-scientists, and how both parents felt unaccepted in their new class and never shared their deepest feelings.

title: Confronting Her Mother's Racism
summary: After she and her brother hosted a party for YMCA-camp counselors including a Black Vassar student, she found her mother weeping in the basement over the visit, a slur on her lips, a moment that crystallized the contradiction in her mother's prejudice.

title: Race and Religion in a White Town
summary: She reflects on the near-total absence of Black students in her schooling, her confusion about religious difference, and a phase of fervent Catholicism that ended in a Cornell confessional when a priest condemned her for reading Locke.

title: A Godmother and a Mother's Isolation
summary: Garman Robinson tells how her Mexican godmother Lucy Droz became part of the family yet drew her mother's contempt, reading the episode as a sign of her mother's deep isolation and her reflexive fear of communism.

title: Finding Politics at Skidmore
summary: At Skidmore she stumbled into the National Student Association, and the 1958 National Student Congress, where students debated apartheid and segregation, was the life-changing experience that turned her from a psychology to a political science major.

title: First Campaigns and the Loyalty Oath
topic: Student Voice and the Loyalty Oath
summary: As National Student Association chairman she took up campus democracy and the fight against the National Defense Education Act loyalty oath, applying the civil-liberties course she had taken and learning the citizen's role in a democracy.

title: Supporting the Sit-Ins at Saratoga
summary: When the 1960 sit-ins broke out, four heated campus meetings led 200 Skidmore women to march on the local Woolworth's, and the first students sent to picket the next day were taken to the police station.

title: Shut Down, and the Road Not Taken to Shaw
summary: The college president shut the protest down and spring break dissipated its energy, and though she considered attending the Shaw University meeting that founded SNCC that same break, fear of her mother's reaction held her back.

title: Awakening to White Privilege
summary: Garman Robinson describes how the courage of the sit-in students pulled the cover off the country's contradictions for her, exposing the white privileges, GI Bill, FHA and VA loans, she had never noticed, and recalls Ella Baker's reminder that it was more than a hamburger.

title: The CIA Behind the Student Association
summary: Recruited after graduation into the International Student Relations Seminar, she later understood it as a training ground for National Student Association officers and the CIA, whose international program quietly funneled student travelers' notes on foreign movements to the agency.

title: Witting: How the Vetting Worked
summary: She explains how the CIA planted agents in the seminars, vetted officers for sympathy with US foreign policy, and let them in on the secret with the code word witting, and how Tim Jenkins, distrusted, was maneuvered from international to national vice president.

title: The Algerian Students and California
summary: At the 1960 Minnesota congress she was swept up by the energy of visiting Algerian revolutionary students, then took a job as a National Student Association campus traveler selling Europe tours up and down the West Coast.

title: Meeting SDS, and Casey Hayden
summary: Back in Philadelphia she organized the Wisconsin student congress and met the SDS circle around Al Haber and Tom Hayden in the run-up to Port Huron, and remembers the electric charge of Casey Hayden stepping out on a panel supporting the sit-ins.

title: Recording Resumes: The Early Years Retold
summary: After a recording break the interview restarts from the top, and Garman Robinson retells her professor father, her prejudiced mother, the YMCA-camp incident, and her break from Catholicism, before the conversation moves forward to 1963.

title: The Singing at the Howard Meeting
summary: Her strongest memory of the 1963 Thanksgiving SNCC meeting at Howard is the singing, a circle of freedom songs and tears at We Shall Overcome that gave her the emotional connection and courage she had not grown up with.

title: Helsinki, and Choosing SNCC Over SDS
topic: World Youth Festival and the SDS Split
summary: She recounts the CIA-funded 1962 World Youth Festival in Helsinki, where American students were sent as an anti-communist alternative to the Soviet bloc line, and the bitter anti-communism of the League for Industrial Democracy that fractured early SDS.

title: Affirmation in SNCC
summary: Recalling the screaming LID-SDS split and her own wallflower silence among SDS men, she explains that SNCC was where she was affirmed, by James Forman and Casey Hayden, given concrete work, and drawn by an organizing energy SDS lacked.

title: Coming South to the Atlanta Office
summary: She contrasts the male-dominated SDS with SNCC, then describes coming South in March 1964 to work alongside Dinky Romilly as Northern Coordinator and going to the Greenwood office for Freedom Summer.

title: Inside the Atlanta Office
summary: Garman Robinson paints the Atlanta office as a dedicated family of support workers like Wilson Brown and Willie McCray, and explains the WATS line and the collect-call operator tricks that let projects report in twice a day while dodging phone charges.

title: The Southern Student Freedom Fund
summary: She describes the Southern Student Freedom Fund, a coalition of four student organizations that raised money for expelled students, bail, and field secretaries, and underscores how black students drove the work forward.

title: Privilege, and a Family's Alarm
topic: Black Leadership and Her Own Privilege
summary: She affirms that black students led and sacrificed in ways she as a Northerner with a degree did not, and recounts her family's conviction that she had been brainwashed even as they shipped an air conditioner to the sweltering Greenwood office.

title: Where She Lived and What She Was Paid
summary: Garman Robinson recalls the integrated and Black freedom houses she moved through in Atlanta, the question of who paid the rent, and SNCC's subsistence pay of nine dollars and sixty-four cents a week that rose to forty dollars for Atlanta staff.

title: The Work of the Northern Coordinator
summary: As Northern Coordinator she wrote handwritten thank-you notes for unsolicited donations and coordinated the Friends of SNCC offices, relaying daily reports from McComb, Greenwood, Albany, and Selma and channeling them into rallies and press calls, all without any science of messaging.

title: Documenting the Atrocities
summary: She details how the office paid the projects, documented the daily brutality of each community, and coordinated with the NAACP Legal Defense Fund, SCLC, CORE, and church bodies, taking up fights over the ASCS farm subsidies and school desegregation under the Civil Rights Act.

title: The Print Shop and the Fundraising Appeal
summary: Garman Robinson describes the Atlanta print shop and the Student Voice, the late-night envelope-stuffing parties, and a fundraising appeal that framed the movement as a fight for democratic ideals rather than charity for the poor.

title: Into the Field at Greenwood
summary: She recalls co-editing Hands on the Freedom Plow decades later, her pull toward the field even while safe in the office, and going canvassing for voter registration in Greenwood with summer volunteer Peter Orris.

title: The Federal Programs Investigation
summary: Taking up federal-programs work in 1965, she traveled to Arkansas to collect affidavits on discriminatory benefits and, with Marion Barry, combed the Office of Education's files in Washington, exposing the dispensations granted to foot-dragging school districts in a report presented with John Lewis.

title: After Atlantic City: Soul-Searching and Selma
summary: She frames the rejection of the MFDP delegation as a turning point that sent SNCC into deep soul-searching over the Black Belt summer project, local organizing, and federal benefits, just as Selma erupted and SNCC chafed at Dr. King's arrival.

title: The Plane to Selma and the Car Fleet
summary: After the Edmund Pettus Bridge attack she chartered a plane to rush staff to Selma, and she recalls the forty-car Sojourner Motor Fleet bought at dealer cost through the United Auto Workers, and the unwritten history of where the movement's money came from.

title: Privilege, Structure, and Funding Fights
summary: She reflects on how white and educational privilege let her carve out the federal-programs role, where she stood in SNCC's structure debate, and the unwritten resentment that Mississippi drew volunteers and money while Georgia, Alabama, and Arkansas felt like stepchildren.

title: Waveland and the Women's Position Paper
summary: Garman Robinson describes the Waveland meeting where papers were written on every theme, including the anonymous women's position paper later tied to Mary King and Casey Hayden, and explains why, feeling affirmed and holding the freedom struggle paramount, she stayed out of women's liberation then.

title: The DC Office, and Leaving SNCC
summary: Moving full-time to the Washington office, she worked on Free DC and a bus boycott under Marion Barry, felt the staff sidelined by his alliance with an older outsider, and finally quit with a letter protesting that the internal culture had stopped being participatory.

title: Racial Dynamics and the Closest White People
summary: She gives a candid account of SNCC's racial dynamics, the interracial relationships and Black-only parties, the absence of anyone to school white staff on privilege, and her understanding that as the closest white people, organizers like her absorbed a justified Black anger, once a blow on an 18th Street sidewalk.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: Whites in the White Community
summary: Garman Robinson examines why SNCC's push for whites to organize the white community faltered, the harassment such volunteers met, the Southern Student Organizing Committee, and the closed, clan-versus-us thinking that kept activists inside their own beloved community of struggle.

title: SURJ and Black Lives Matter
summary: She traces Showing Up for Racial Justice from its 2009 origins through Trayvon Martin and Ferguson to its accountability work with Black Lives Matter, and her own door-knocking with Black Lives Matter signs after the 2015 Baltimore uprising.

title: Highlander
summary: Visiting Highlander at last in 2015, she recounts its 1930s roots bringing Black and white workers together, its citizenship education with Septima Clark and Rosa Parks, and the repression that forced it to move.

title: The Years Between: Factory Work and Public Health
summary: Garman Robinson sketches the four decades after SNCC, a Black community arts program, the anti-war and women's movements, eight years in a Baltimore factory, taking medical students on factory tours, asbestos screening, and fourteen years at Johns Hopkins public health.

title: A Return to Organizing
summary: The organizing bug returned through the National Organizers Alliance, leading her to a lead-organizer job in Baltimore neighborhood and anti-crime work and to writing community-college curriculum aimed at growing the number of organizers of color.

title: Closing Reflections
summary: In closing she reflects on what a movement is and how briefly its window stays open, the duty of the white community to put its resources behind Black-led organizations, the value of scholarship and history, and her conviction that the Black struggle must stay at the center.
